text: Ironically today that  use-case is now de-favored by the algorithm.  Heavily.  So you have to jump through hoops to share links.   If you want anyone to see them, that is.

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
I’m not a huge Web 3.0 guy, but I’ve been reading Chris Dixon’s “Read, Write Own” and his analysis of “what went wrong” with the Internet is spot on. 

I’m not all sure that Web 3.0 fixes all those problems, but the problems are real.

name: Dave Kellogg
text: This is not some general nihilism.  It’s a property of social media.  First the early adopters come and with a vision.  Then some breakthrough happens and it takes off.  Then everyone comes.  Then it gets ruined.

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
I went on Facebook hiatus several years ago.  

The trigger event was that I found myself in an argument with a moron from my high school.  An argument, btw, that I was very politely trying to not have.  

I asked myself:  why I am spending my time this way?  Is this a good use

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
Put differently.  I didn’t change.  The site changed on me.  I still wanted to do the use-case I’ve always done.  But the site isn’t about that use-case anymore.

Dance with who brung ya. 

I’m sure some other site will come along and do that, so is the Silicon Valley cycle of

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
I saw a post on Instagram the other day.  

The person was saying:  I used this site to keep in touch with my friends.  To see updates on them and their kids.  Now I can’t find those updates.  The more the site became “entertainment,” the more I got sucked into viral videos,

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
Social media is a tragedy of the commons.  For that matter, so is the Internet.  The trend seems to be that it gets ruined over time.

I remember using eBay back when people always paid, would come to my house to pick up items.

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
The deterioration is two-sided.

Not only is it harder to share things that people will find, it’s harder to find things that I want to share.

So my two use-cases were:
1. Share my content.  Now harder and less effective.
2. Find interesting things to read.  Now harder and less

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
For Twitter, I think the breakthrough was link-shortening.  Unlike Delicious, which was cumbersome to use, Twitter became an easy way to share links to interesting things.  

Casting URLs as messages in bottles beat social bookmarking.

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
Some of this is a crisis of measurement.

I never understood why “engagement” mattered so much to social media companies.  (I also never really understood “getting ratioed.” )

If you view Twitter as a link-sharing site, the dominant metric should be links pressed.  

But likes

name: Dave Kellogg
text:
And never came back.  And don’t miss it.  

I don’t know how my Twitter/X hiatus will end.  I’ve really enjoyed the site over the years.  I thank all of you who’ve followed me, and engaged with me.  

I’ve found new friends and new companies here.

I’m just no confident that will
